title: Oculomotor (CN III) palsy: efferent pupillary defect + ptosis
vignette: A 34-year-old woman comes to the office due to double vision. The patient first experienced difficulty focusing her eyes while at work yesterday. On awakening this morning, her symptoms had progressed to frank diplopia. Medical history is insignificant. A complete physical examination is performed, including a full neurologic assessment. Shining light into the patient's right eye causes constriction of her right pupil, but not the left pupil. Shining light into her left eye causes constriction of only her right pupil. Which of the following additional physical examination findings is likely to be found in this patient?

clues.note: Diplopia signals an extraocular-muscle/cranial-nerve problem. With the pupil findings below, it localizes to CN III, which moves most extraocular muscles and carries the pupillary constrictor fibers.

clues.text: Shining light into the patient's right eye causes constriction of her right pupil, but not the left pupil
clues.note: Right light → right pupil constricts (intact right afferent + right efferent = direct response) but the LEFT pupil does not (absent consensual). The failure is on the LEFT efferent (motor) limb — the left pupil cannot constrict to any stimulus.

clues.text: Shining light into her left eye causes constriction of only her right pupil
clues.note: Left light → the RIGHT pupil constricts (left afferent is intact, driving the right consensual response), but the LEFT pupil still doesn't constrict. So the left eye SEES light (afferent OK) but cannot CONSTRICT — a left EFFERENT pupillary defect = left CN III lesion. CN III also raises the eyelid, so expect left ptosis.

whatMatters: Decode the pupils: the LEFT pupil never constricts to light entering either eye, while the left eye still drives the right pupil — that's a left EFFERENT defect = left CN III palsy. CN III also innervates the levator palpebrae, so the expected extra finding is left eyelid drooping (ptosis) — answer B.
strategy: For pupillary-reflex puzzles, separate afferent (CN II, 'does the eye SEE the light?') from efferent (CN III, 'can the pupil CONSTRICT?'). A pupil that won't constrict to light from EITHER eye = efferent (CN III) defect. Then recall CN III's other jobs: ptosis, 'down-and-out' eye, and (if compressive) a blown pupil.

options.text: Absence of the left corneal reflex
Inability to close the left eye
Inward deviation of the left eye
Visual loss in the left eye

eliminations.label: absent corneal reflex — CN V1/VII, not III
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the corneal reflex tests CN V1 (afferent) and CN VII (efferent blink). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: this patient's lesion is CN III (efferent pupil + extraocular muscles), which doesn't mediate the corneal reflex. ✅ Correct if: the lesion involved trigeminal V1 or facial VII.

eliminations.label: can't close the eye — facial nerve (CN VII)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: orbicularis oculi closes the eye and is CN VII. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: CN III OPENS the lid (levator palpebrae); a III lesion causes drooping (ptosis), not failure to close. ✅ Correct if: the deficit were facial-nerve weakness (Bell palsy).

eliminations.label: inward (medial) deviation — that's CN VI, opposite of CN III (your pick)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: an eye deviated INWARD (esotropia) at rest occurs with lateral rectus (CN VI) palsy. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: a CN III palsy gives the OPPOSITE — a 'down and OUT' eye (unopposed lateral rectus + superior oblique), not inward deviation. ✅ Correct if: the lesion were CN VI (abducens).

eliminations.label: visual loss — that's CN II (afferent)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: monocular visual loss implies an optic-nerve (afferent) problem. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: the left eye SEES fine (it drove the right pupil's consensual response); the defect is efferent (CN III), so vision is intact. ✅ Correct if: the left eye failed to drive ANY constriction (afferent/optic-nerve lesion → Marcus Gunn pupil).

reasoning: Left pupil won't constrict to light in either eye = left efferent defect
Left eye still drives the right pupil → left afferent (CN II) intact
Efferent pupillary defect = left CN III (oculomotor) palsy
CN III also elevates the lid → expect left ptosis → answer B
mechanism: CN III (oculomotor) carries parasympathetic fibers to the pupillary sphincter and innervates the levator palpebrae and four extraocular muscles (superior/inferior/medial rectus, inferior oblique). A left CN III lesion blocks the efferent limb so the left pupil cannot constrict to light entering either eye (afferent/CN II is intact, so the left eye still drives the right consensual response). Other CN III signs: ptosis (levator weakness), a 'down-and-out' eye, and — with compressive lesions (PCom aneurysm, uncal herniation) — a dilated, fixed pupil from peripheral parasympathetic fiber involvement.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Neuro > Pupillary light reflex and CN III: afferent (CN II) vs efferent (CN III) limbs, direct/consensual responses, Marcus Gunn (afferent) vs efferent defect; CN III functions and palsy signs (ptosis, down-and-out, blown pupil).

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Work the reflex logic: which pupil fails to constrict regardless of where light enters? That side's EFFERENT (CN III) is out. Then list CN III's other jobs to pick the extra finding.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on afferent vs efferent pupillary defect, consensual response, ptosis, levator palpebrae, 'down and out,' Marcus Gunn pupil? Tell me which and I'll explain.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Inward deviation (D, your pick) is the CN VI sign — the exact opposite of CN III's 'down and OUT.' Don't swap the abducens and oculomotor pictures.

difficulty.label: 🖼️ Imaging / diagram
difficulty.message: The CN table maps each nerve's muscles, functions, and lesion findings. Practice the pupillary-reflex circuit diagram (light → CN II → pretectal → Edinger-Westphal → CN III → sphincter) to localize afferent vs efferent defects.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Missed as D → drill the contrast: CN III = ptosis + down-and-out + dilated pupil; CN VI = inward deviation (can't abduct). They look opposite.

keywords.front: Pupil won't constrict to light from either eye
keywords.back: Efferent (CN III) defect on that side

keywords.front: Eye sees light but pupil can't constrict
keywords.back: Efferent CN III lesion (afferent CN II intact)
Ptosis, 'down and out' eye, dilated pupil (if compressive)
SR, IR, MR, IO + levator palpebrae + pupillary sphincter (parasympathetic)

keywords.front: Compressive vs ischemic CN III palsy
keywords.back: Compressive (PCom aneurysm) → pupil involved; ischemic (diabetes) → pupil spared

firstAid.detail: Neuro: CN III functions, pupillary light reflex efferent limb, ptosis/down-and-out, compressive vs ischemic (pupil-sparing) palsies.
firstAid.location: Neuro: CN III functions, pupillary light reflex efferent limb, ptosis/down-and-out, compressive vs ischemic (pupil-sparing) palsies.

firstAid.detail: Neuro: CN II (vision/afferent), V1+VII (corneal reflex), VI (lateral rectus/abduction), VII (eye closure).
firstAid.location: Neuro: CN II (vision/afferent), V1+VII (corneal reflex), VI (lateral rectus/abduction), VII (eye closure).

firstAid.detail: Neuro: retina → CN II → pretectal nucleus → bilateral Edinger-Westphal → CN III → ciliary ganglion → sphincter; direct vs consensual.
firstAid.location: Neuro: retina → CN II → pretectal nucleus → bilateral Edinger-Westphal → CN III → ciliary ganglion → sphincter; direct vs consensual.

memoryHook: The left pupil is 'deaf to all light' = its MOTOR (CN III) is out. CN III also lifts the lid → left ptosis (and 'down & out').
